other_observers: Janeal Thompson, Jane Stulp, Cole Sage, Luke Pheneger, Pablo Quezada
description: Warbler that was whitish below, medium gray above.  The head was mostly red with a large black middle portion that extended from the cap down thru the eye, coming to a point at the bottom (see photos).  The initial report by Steve Mlodinow, and a later email from Glenn Walbeck, indicated when they observed it ealier in the day the bird was foraging fairly low in a small group of mid-sized trees just beginning to leaf out.  When Jane, Janeal and I finally found the bird, it was in the tops of plains cottonwood trees.  The aforementioned mostly-leafless trees were mulberries.  We never saw the bird in the mulberries.  I think the reason the bird moved in early evening to the tops of the cottonwoods was because the crowns at that time of day were still bathed in sunlight and insects were still active in the catkins and high-up cottonwood leaves.  I would describe the activity level of the bird as modest (more active than a typical vireo, less hyper than kinglets or some warblers can be).   The bird was silent as far as we could tell. The redness of the head seemed more than what a female would be.
photographer: Photographer: David A. Leatherman, David A. Leatherman, David A. Leatherman
